1: Bezalel made the ark (aron) of acacia wood: The shift from plural construction agents (wayyaasu = they made, in Exodus 36) to singular (wayyaas B'tzal'el = Bezalel made) for the ark is deliberate. Bezalel personally constructs the holiest object of the tabernacle. The aron (from the same root as the Egyptian sarcophagus box) is the throne-base of YHWH — the dwelling of the Name. The ark's acacia-and-gold construction follows the q'rashin principle: imperishable wood covered in pure gold.
2: He overlaid it with pure gold (zahav tahor) inside and outside, and made a molding of gold around it: Zahav tahor = pure gold (tahor = pure/clean — the ritual cleanness term applied to metal quality); inside and outside (mibayit umichutz) — both surfaces overlaid. The gold molding (zer zahav = golden wreath/crown around the top) may symbolize the divine crown-presence that rests on the ark. The ark is completely enclosed in gold — no wood visible; the divine presence is clothed entirely in purity.
3: And he cast for it four rings of gold for its four feet, two rings on its one side and two rings on its other side: The four gold rings at the four feet (or lower corners — the exact position is debated) are the permanent housing for the poles. The ark's transportation system is built into its design — it was never meant to be stationary. The portable ark (carried on Levitical shoulders, never touched by hands) is the mobile throne of the divine King who travels with his people.
4: He made poles (badim) of acacia wood and overlaid them with gold: Badim = poles (singular bad = separation/isolation — a pole that separates the bearers from the ark); the gold-covered acacia poles are the means of carrying the holiest object in Israel. The poles must never be removed (Exodus 25:15 — 'The poles shall remain in the rings of the ark; they shall not be taken from it') because the ark is always ready to move, always in a state of divine readiness to journey.
5: And he put the poles into the rings on the sides of the ark to carry the ark: The final assembly of the ark — poles inserted through the rings, the ark itself completed. The ark will not be used until the tabernacle is erected (Exodus 40:20); until then it stands ready, poles in place, waiting for the dwelling to be built around it.
6: He made a mercy seat (kapporeth) of pure gold: Kapporeth = the mercy seat (from kipper = to atone/cover; the word also appears in Exodus 25:17-22). Paul uses the LXX hilasterion (= kapporeth) for Christ in Romans 3:25 — Jesus is the kapporeth, the place where atoning blood is applied and where divine wrath and divine mercy intersect. Two and a half cubits was its length, and one and a half cubits its breadth: The kapporeth exactly covers the ark (same dimensions as the ark's lid), and the cherubim are hammered from the same piece of gold as the kapporeth.
7: And he made two cherubim of gold (k'ruvei zahav). He made them of hammered work (miqqshah) on the two ends of the mercy seat: Miqqshah = hammered/beaten work (from qashah = hard/difficult to work; the same root used for Pharaoh's hardened heart — the material is worked with difficulty, requiring skill and sustained force). The cherubim are not cast separately and attached; they are hammered out of the same piece of gold as the kapporeth — one continuous piece. The organic unity of the cherubim with the mercy seat reflects the inseparability of the guardians of YHWH's presence from the presence itself.
8: One cherub on the one end and one cherub on the other end. Of one piece (miqqshah) with the mercy seat he made the cherubim on its two ends: The emphasis on one piece (miqqshah echad, lit. one hammered work) is repeated from Exodus 37:7. The artistic and theological point: the kapporeth and its cherubim are not assembled from parts but formed as an integral whole. The artisan who could hammer a cherub and a flat cover from a single gold ingot was demonstrating extraordinary skill — the finest metalwork in the tabernacle.
9: The cherubim spread out their wings above, overshadowing the mercy seat (s'khakhim b'khnapheim al-hakkapporeth) with their wings, with their faces one to another (peneihem ish el-achiv); toward the mercy seat were the faces of the cherubim: Sakhakh = to overshadow/cover (from the same root as sukkah = booth/shelter; the cherubim provide protective covering for the ark). Peneihem ish el-achiv = each facing his brother — the mutual gaze of the cherubim over the mercy seat; they do not look outward at observers but inward at the locus of atonement. Between them and beneath their gaze, YHWH promises to speak (Exodus 25:22 — 'I will speak with you from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubim'). The John 20:12 angels at the tomb echo this arrangement.
10: He also made the table (shulchan) of acacia wood: Shulchan = table (the word also used for the royal table at a feast; cf. Psalms 23:5 — 'You prepare a table before me'); the table of the Presence (Bread) stands in the Holy Place on the north side. The table-in-the-divine-house continues the royal throne-room imagery of the tabernacle — the ark (throne), the incense altar (before the throne), and the table (the king's banquet provision) map onto a royal court.
11: He overlaid it with pure gold and made a molding of gold around it: The table, like the ark and the incense altar, has a golden molding (zer zahav = golden wreath/crown); the three innermost pieces of furniture all have this crown-molding. The molding may represent a royal coronet — the furniture of the divine court is crowned.
12: He also made a frame (misgeret) around it a handbreadth wide, and a molding of gold around the frame: Misgeret = frame/border/rim (from sagar = to close/shut); the handbreadth (tefach) rim prevents the showbread and its vessels from sliding off. The double molding (one on the table, one on the rim) multiplies the royal crown imagery.
13: He cast for it four rings of gold and fastened the rings to the four corners at its four legs: The table, like the ark, has four gold rings for carrying poles; it is designed for transit. Even the table of the divine Presence travels with Israel — the banquet provision follows the pilgrim people.
14: Close to the frame were the rings, as holders for the poles to carry the table: The rings are positioned close to the frame (the lateral edge) rather than at the feet — this positions the carrying poles outside the table legs, making it easier for the bearers to walk without tripping on the legs.
15: And he made the poles of acacia wood to carry the table, and overlaid them with gold: The table's acacia-and-gold poles mirror the ark's poles in construction. All carrying implements follow the same material protocol as the object they carry: the holiness of the object extends to its transportation apparatus.
16: And he made the vessels of pure gold that were to be on the table, its plates (q'arot) and dishes (kappot) and bowls (q'sayyot) and flagons (m'naqqiyot) with which to pour drink offerings: The complete service set for the table of the Presence: q'arot = flat plates (for the twelve loaves); kappot = hands/pans (perhaps for incense placed alongside the bread); q'sayyot = bowls (for the frankincense? Leviticus 24:7); m'naqqiyot = flagons (for the drink offerings — liquid libations). All of pure gold (tahor); the vessels are as holy as the table itself.
17: He also made the lampstand (menorah) of pure gold (tahor). He made the lampstand of hammered work (miqqshah): The menorah receives the same material quality standard (tahor = pure/clean gold) and construction method (miqqshah = hammered) as the cherubim and mercy seat — the three most technically demanding pieces of the tabernacle. The menorah is one continuous hammered piece (no joints or welds), requiring the goldsmith to hammer the entire structure including its branches from a single gold ingot. Its base (yarekhah), its shaft (qaneh), its cups (gevi'im), its calyxes (kaptorim), and its flowers (perachim) were of one piece with it (miqqshah hayetah kullah): Yarekhah = thigh/base; qaneh = stalk/reed; gevi'im = cups; kaptorim = knobs/calyxes (from Caphtor, the Aegean origin of the design?); perachim = blossoms/flowers. The botanical vocabulary (cups, calyxes, flowers) is the vocabulary of the almond tree (shaqed).
18: And there were six branches going out of its sides, three branches of the lampstand out of one side of it and three branches of the lampstand out of the other side of it: The seven-branched menorah: one central shaft (qaneh) + six branches (shnei q'nei menorah = six branches of the lampstand) = seven lights total. Seven = the complete week, the Sabbath number. The menorah provides the only light in the Holy Place (there are no windows); its seven lights illuminate the space where priests minister before YHWH.
19: Three cups made like almond blossoms (meshukadim), each with calyx and flower, on one branch, and three cups made like almond blossoms, each with calyx and flower, on the other branch: Meshukadim = shaped like almonds/almond blossoms (from shaqed = almond; the same root gives the verb shakad = to watch/be alert — used in Jeremiah 1:11-12, 'I see an almond branch... for I am watching/shakad over my word to perform it'; the almond is the early-bloomer, the watcher-tree). Three cups per branch = the flowering sequence: calyx (kaptor = bud), petal (perach = flower), open cup (gavia' = cup). The menorah is an almond tree in gold, always in full bloom.
20: And on the lampstand itself were four cups made like almond blossoms, with their calyxes and flowers: The central shaft has four almond cups (vs. three on each branch), making the central shaft the most elaborately flowering element. Total cups: (3 × 6 branches) + (4 × central shaft) = 22 cups of almond-blossom design.
21: And there was a calyx of one piece with it under each pair of the six branches of it: At the junction of each pair of branches where they emerge from the central shaft, there is a knob/calyx of one piece — structural reinforcement at the branching points. The junctions are where fracture would most likely occur in a hammered piece; the calyx both strengthens and beautifies the branching points.
22: Their calyxes and their branches were of one piece with it (miqqshah echad). The whole of it was a single hammered piece (miqqshah echad) of pure gold: The most emphatic restatement of the menorah's unity: miqqshah echad (one hammered piece) stated twice in this verse. The entire menorah — base, shaft, branches, cups, calyxes, flowers — is one continuous piece of worked gold. This is the material embodiment of the 'one' that runs through the tabernacle construction: the mishkan is one (Exodus 36:13), the mercy seat and cherubim are one piece (Exodus 37:8), the menorah is one piece.
23: He made its seven lamps (neroteha) and its tongs (malqacheim) and its trays (machtoteha) of pure gold: Nerot = lamps (from ner = lamp/light); malqachayim = tongs/snuffers (for trimming the wicks); machtot = fire-pans/trays (for catching the trimmed wicks and ash). The maintenance tools are as pure as the menorah itself — the instruments of daily service share the holiness standard of the object they service.
24: He made it and all its utensils of a talent (kikkar) of pure gold: Kikkar = a talent (the largest unit of weight; approximately 75 pounds/34 kg of gold). The menorah required one full talent of gold — the largest single-piece gold object in the tabernacle. The investment of an entire talent of gold in a single hammered lamp is a statement: illuminating the presence of YHWH is worth the most precious material available.
25: He made the altar of incense (mizbeach hamiqtar qetoret) of acacia wood: Miqtar = a place of burning/smoking (from qatar = to make smoke); qetoret = incense/smoke-offering. The incense altar (also called mizbeach ha-zahav, the gold altar) is the fourth piece of furniture in the Holy Place, positioned directly before the parokhet. It is the last object encountered before the Most Holy Place — the piece of furniture that mediates between the priest's ministry in the Holy Place and the divine presence beyond the veil.
26: He overlaid it with pure gold, its top (gago), its sides (kirotav), and its horns (qanotav), and made a molding of gold around it: Gag = roof/top (also the word for a house roof); the golden crown-molding (zer) around the incense altar matches the ark and the table, marking it as one of the three furniture pieces requiring the crown. Its horns (qanotav): The incense altar has four horns like the burnt-offering altar, but smaller and of gold rather than bronze. Blood of atonement (on the Day of Atonement and other occasions) is applied to the horns of the incense altar, linking it structurally to the blood atonement system despite its primary incense function.
27: And he made two rings of gold for it under its molding, on two opposite sides of it, as holders for the poles to carry it: The incense altar has two rings (not four like the ark and table), one on each of its two sides. The smaller number of rings for a smaller piece — the incense altar is proportionally smaller than the table and ark (1 cubit × 1 cubit × 2 cubits high, vs. the ark's 2.5 × 1.5 × 1.5 cubits).
28: And he made the poles of acacia wood and overlaid them with gold: Same construction protocol as all other poles: acacia + gold overlay. The incense altar's poles, like all carrying implements, share the material dignity of the object they carry.
29: He made the holy anointing oil also, and the pure fragrant incense (q'toret sammim), blended as by a perfumer (ma'aseh roqeach): The chapter concludes with the two substances (oil and incense) that serve as sensory mediators of divine presence. Shemen mishchat-qodesh = holy anointing oil (used to consecrate every piece of furniture and every priest — the physical transfer of holy status through oil contact). Q'toret sammim = fragrant incense (the specific compound formula of Exodus 30:34-38); ma'aseh roqeach = the work of a perfumer (from raqach = to compound/spice) — the same technical designation as the embroiderer and the jeweler; sacred manufacture requires professional expertise.
